facts.text: Stony Brook’s name comes from the native word Wopowog, meaning “the place of many streams,” a nod to the brook that runs through the town.
The historic Stony Brook Grist Mill, listed on the National Register of Historic Places, is Long Island’s most complete working grist mill and dates back to 1798.
Stony Brook University, founded in 1957, grew from a small college into a major research institution that now hosts the Staller Center for the Arts on campus.
The Long Island Museum of American Art, History, and Carriages, a Smithsonian affiliate, holds over 40,000 items, including nearly 200 historic carriages.
Stony Brook’s tree‑lined streets and well‑preserved homes give the town a New England village feel, a contrast to its modern research hub identity.
The town’s harbor, once a busy shipbuilding center in the 1700s, now serves as a scenic waterfront that anchors Stony Brook’s historic core.
